perturbation.original_text: 13.3 The Receiving Party shall comply with a re quest, in terms of this clause 13 , within 10 (t en) days of receipt of such a request.
perturbation.changed_text: 13.3 The Receiving Party shall comply with a request, in terms of this clause 13, within 2 (two) days of receipt of such a request.
perturbation.explanation: The clause originally allows 10 days for compliance after a request to return confidential information. Shortening this period to 2 days creates an unreasonable burden and contradicts typical standards for document retrieval in legal contexts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Rule 34(b)(2)(A) of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 34(b)(2)(A) requires a response to a request for production of documents (which would include confidential information covered by this NDA) within 30 days after being served, unless the parties stipulate to a different time. The modified clause's 2-day deadline is significantly shorter and thus contradicts the federal rule.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 34. Producing Documents, Electronically Stored Information, and Tangible Things, or Entering onto Land, for Inspection and Other Purposes | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 34. Producing Documents, Electronically Stored Information, and Tangible Things, or Entering onto Land, for Inspection and Other Purposes (a) In General. A party may serve on any other party a request within the scope of Rule 26(b) : (1) to produce and permit the requesting party or its representative to inspect, copy, test, or sample the following items in the responding party's possession, custody, or control: (A) any designated documents or electronically stored information—including writings, drawings, graphs, charts, photographs, sound recordings, images, and other data or data compilations—stored in any medium from which information can be obtained either directly or, if necessary, after translation by the responding party into a reasonably usable form; or (B) any designated tangible things; or (2) to permit entry onto designated land or other property possessed or controlled by the responding party, so that the requesting party may inspect, measure, survey, photograph, test, or sample the property or any designated object or operation on it. (b) Procedure. (1) Contents of the Request. The request: (A) must describe with reasonable particularity each item or category of items to be inspected; (B) must specify a reasonable time, place, and manner for the inspection and for performing the related acts; and (C) may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (2) Responses and Objections. (A) Time to Respond. The party to whom the request is directed must respond in writing within 30 days after being served or — if the request was delivered under Rule 26(d)(2) — within 30 days after the parties’ first Rule 26(f) conference. A shorter or longer time may be s
perturbation.justification: The modified text shortens the compliance period from 10 days to 2 days, contradicting FRCP 34(b)(2)(A), which requires a response within 30 days unless otherwise stipulated. The scraped snippet from the Legal Information Institute states that the party to whom the request is directed must respond in writing within 30 days after being served. The modified contract violates this rule by mandating a response in 2 days, creating a direct conflict with federal procedure and potentially prejudicing the receiving party's ability to adequately respond to the request.

perturbation.original_text: 19. DURATION The confidentiality undertaking as set out herein shall commence on the earliest of the first date on which any of the Parties released any Confidential Information to the other Party or the Ef fective Date and shall endure for a period of 10 (ten) years thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: 19. DURATION The confidentiality undertaking as set out herein shall commence on the earliest of the first date on which any of the Parties released any Confidential Information to the other Party or the Effective Date and shall endure perpetually.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the duration of confidentiality to 'perpetually' introduces a potential contradiction with trade secret law. While trade secrets can be protected indefinitely, this protection depends on the information remaining secret and providing a competitive advantage. A perpetual confidentiality clause may be challenged if the information loses its trade secret status.
perturbation.law_explanation: UTSA § 2(b) allows for injunctive relief to prevent misappropriation of a trade secret. However, the underlying premise is that the information *is* a trade secret.  A perpetual confidentiality agreement might be challenged if the information becomes public knowledge or no longer provides a competitive advantage, meaning it no longer meets the definition of a trade secret.  While not a direct contradiction, the 'perpetual' nature goes against the fundamental principles of what constitutes a trade secret and the limits on its protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the confidentiality duration to 'perpetually.' The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest this could contradict trade secret law, as trade secret protection depends on the information remaining secret and providing a competitive advantage. A perpetual confidentiality clause might be challenged if the information loses its trade secret status, as the 'perpetual' nature goes against the principles of what constitutes a trade secret.

perturbation.original_text: 20.2 If the Senior Executives cannot agree on a resolution of the Dispute within 15 Business Days of receipt of the notice referred to in clause 19.1, then the Dispute may be referred within a further period of 20 B usiness Days (Arbitration Initiating Period) to arbitration in accordance with this clause 19 unless the Parties agree in writing prior to the expiry of the Arbitration Initiating Period to refer such Dispute for expert determination in accordance with thi s clause 19.
perturbation.changed_text: 20.2 If the Senior Executives cannot agree on a resolution of the Dispute within 3 Business Days of receipt of the notice referred to in clause 19.1, then the Dispute may be referred within a further period of 5 Business Days (Arbitration Initiating Period) to arbitration in accordance with this clause 19 unless the Parties agree in writing prior to the expiry of the Arbitration Initiating Period to refer such Dispute for expert determination in accordance with this clause 19.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the negotiation and arbitration initiating periods to 3 and 5 business days, respectively, could be deemed unconscionable and in bad faith, conflicting with general principles of contract law regarding fairness and reasonable opportunity to resolve disputes, particularly in South Africa where the contract is governed.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 48 of the Consumer Protection Act 68 of 2008 (South Africa)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 48 of the Consumer Protection Act 68 of 2008 in South Africa prohibits unfair, unreasonable, or unjust contract terms. While this agreement is B2B, drastically reducing the time for dispute resolution can be argued as unconscionable, particularly if it advantages one party significantly over the other, thus contravening the spirit of fairness encouraged by the CPA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Page Not Found | GovInfo Skip to main content Page Not Found Help Error occurred. The page you requested cannot be found. Please report this error to askGPO. Provide the following information to help us resolve this problem: the URL of the page you were trying to access, the steps you followed to produce the error, specific search or browse terms, and/or a screenshot of the page where the error occurred. Thank you for your patience. Homepage Search Tips Skip back to top
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the negotiation and arbitration initiating periods from 15 and 20 business days to 3 and 5 business days, respectively. The Law Explanation states that this drastic reduction in time for dispute resolution can be argued as unconscionable and contravenes the spirit of fairness. The Perturbation Explanation states that this could be deemed unconscionable and in bad faith, conflicting with general principles of contract law regarding fairness and reasonable opportunity to resolve disputes. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, where time-sensitive obligations do not align with legal requirements. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 results in a 404 error and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is 'None', the combination of the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation, compared to the original and changed text, is sufficient evidence of a potential legal contradiction. The change potentially violates the principles of fairness and reasonable opportunity, making the contractual terms potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 21.3.3 if transmitted by facsimile be deemed to have been received by the addres see 1 (one) day after despatch.
perturbation.changed_text: 21.3.3 if transmitted by facsimile be deemed to have been received by the addressee immediately upon despatch, regardless of whether a confirmation receipt is received.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the notice provision to deem faxed notices as received immediately, regardless of confirmation, contradicts established legal standards. It puts the onus on the receiving party to prove non-receipt, even without confirmation from their end.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Electronic Communications and Transactions Act, 2002 (South Africa), Section 23(b)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 23(b) of the ECTA states that an expression of intent (such as a notice) is deemed to be received when the complete data message enters an information system designated or used for that purpose by the addressee *and* is capable of being retrieved and processed by the addressee.  The changed clause disregards whether the message is actually capable of being retrieved or processed, thus creating a situation where a notice is deemed received even if the addressee cannot access it, potentially violating the ECTA and principles of fair notice.

perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the principles of fair notice and the reasonable expectation that a notice is actually received, as suggested by the Law Explanation. The change removes the requirement of confirmation, effectively deeming a notice received even if the addressee cannot access it. While no specific law snippet directly addresses this point, the Law Explanation points to Section 23(b) of the ECTA, which implies the need for the message to be retrievable and processable. The modified text undermines this principle, creating a contradiction because it prioritizes immediate despatch over actual receipt and access, potentially violating legal standards for providing adequate notice.
